pattern: Every value up to thirty has a small, fixed footprint of prime factors, so square-free-ness of a subset's product is really just a bitmask no-overlap condition over the ten primes below thirty, countable with a subset-sum style DP.
hints: Values only range from 1 to 30, so first work out which values can never appear in any valid subset at all: any value that is itself divisible by a perfect square.
For every remaining value, encode which of the primes below 30 divide it as a bitmask; a group of values keeps a square-free product exactly when none of their masks share a bit.

Do a DP over the 1024 possible bitmasks of the ten relevant primes, adding each value's count as either 0 or 1 uses (never 2, since repeating a prime factor breaks square-freeness); handle the value 1 separately since it never conflicts with anything and doubles the count of every subset each time it can be freely included or excluded.

edge_inputs.description: several copies of the same value that is always square-free alone
every value is already excluded as not square-free by itself
single value that is not square-free
single value that has several distinct prime factors
duplicate values must count as distinct subsets even though only one copy may ever be chosen
ones mixed with values that can never be chosen
two values that individually are fine but together break square-freeness

stress.description: uniformly random values from the whole allowed range
heavy reuse of a handful of square-free values, stressing the multiplicity counting
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    pool = [1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 10, 15, 30]
    return [[rng.choice(pool) for _ in range(n)]]
